title: AI safety ignored in sprint to go public, critics warn
full_text:
IPOs Super funds are eyeing windfalls as tech giants follow SpaceX.
Australian fund managers and superannuation funds are sprinting towards windfalls as artificial intelligence giants Anthropic and OpenAI join SpaceX in the race to go public. But critics warn that amid the scramble for funding, safety threats from the new technology are being ignored.
Elon Musk's blockbuster SpaceX float this week is the first float of the AI frontier model makers, as he merged xAI, the company behind its Grok platform, into the company in February. OpenAI on Tuesday joined its main rival, Anthropic, in filing for a trillion-dollar listing this year.
Investors and industry watchers worry the three mega-floats will suck all the capital out of the market for other tech companies - such as Australian private company star Canva - and that Anthropic's launch of a "Mythos-class" model overnight on Tuesday showed concerns about AI risks are now running second to revenue growth inside the companies. Mythos is Anthropic's most powerful model, and it raised global concerns in April when, in a scheme called Project Glasswing, the company said it would restrict access to a select group of US companies and agencies because it could quickly discover security flaws in software and other tech infrastructure. Glasswing was extended to Australia last week.
Superannuation giants including AustralianSuper, Hostplus, Cbus and Hesta have access to some of the companies, while fund managers such as Melbourne-based Boman Group secured stakes in previous funding rounds of both Anthropic and OpenAI.
In April, The Australian Financial Review revealed the country's largest superannuation provider, AustralianSuper, owned stakes in all three of the AI companies that will go public.
Hostplus has exposure to OpenAI and Anthropic (but not SpaceX) through international venture capital investments, and filings show Cbus and Hesta have bought into VenCap, a UK-based investment fund of funds with exposure to all the AI companies' earliest investors.
"AI is one of the defining productivity themes of our generation. We've been building considered exposure to it across the portfolio for some years now, across both private and public markets and well before the current wave of listings came into view," said Hostplus chief investment officer Sam Sicilia.
"Our role isn't to predict the timing or pricing of individual IPOs. It's to make sure our members are well positioned in the structural shifts that will shape the global economy over the next two, three and four decades."
Last week, Anthropic urged a global freeze in AI development over concerns that humans could lose control of AI's capabilities.
However, the launch of its new model, Claude Fable 5, has led some local industry watchers to suggest that its call for caution now looks more like a marketing stunt aimed at highlighting its model's prowess to investors.
Anthropic said Fable 5's capabilities exceed those of any model it has made generally available and are equal to Mythos, although guardrails have been applied to prevent it from committing cybercrimes. It boasts increased capabilities in coding, AI agent operation, medical research and cybersecurity, which its own testing indicates trumps OpenAI's GPT-5.5 and Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro models.
Anthropic said Fable 5 would not be a cybersecurity disaster because it had been extensively tested against attempts to jailbreak or bypass its safety guardrails.
"As well as internal testing, we ran an external bug bounty that produced no universal jailbreaks in over 1000 hours of testing," Anthropic said.
Toby Walsh, chief scientist at the UNSW AI Institute, said Anthropic's technology was establishing a lead over OpenAI's, which meant he would be happy from an investment perspective to have his superannuation exposed to its shares. However, he said the demands of public markets do not gel with Anthropic's claims about responsible AI development.
Walsh said SpaceX was being used by Musk to hide and boost the underperforming xAI, much like he had tried to boost Twitter's flagging fortunes by combining it with his AI firm.
But he said he was most concerned by Anthropic's seeming willingness to launch systems it knows could cause huge damage. "Count the hours before someone finds a way to jailbreak these models. There hasn't been a model ever released that wasn't quickly jailbroken ... The only way to be sure that a model can't do something is not to train it to do the thing in the first place," Walsh said.
"More importantly, we haven't fixed all the zero-day flaws that tools like Mythos might uncover ... You would have imagined that they'd have held it back for six months to get on top of this challenge; instead, it's been about six weeks."
Will Liang, the founder of Australian AI advisory firm Amplify AI, has performed an initial test on Fable 5 and said it was "exceptional" at creating PowerPoint decks and better at building websites that "don't feel vibe-coded".
However, he also said he doubted its cyber guardrails would survive public experimentation.
"I am not confident, given that all language models are regularly being jailbroken. I will not be surprised if Fable can be jailbroken soon," he said.
Boman Group's Eric Gao said he thinks there will be enough investor demand to meet the capital-raising targets of Anthropic's and OpenAI's IPOs. But, with no dates specified for the floats, he said it was undeniable that both would want to get there first.
"Does it matter who gets there first? Absolutely. This isn't a league table; it's a race for a pool of capital," Gao said.
"With the market nervous about US interest rates and the macro, the smart move for both is to get to the window quickly and lock in the anchor money before sentiment turns. The first mover sets the narrative and takes first call on the deepest allocations."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Smith
heading: Data Science and AI
